summary:
The Congenital Heart Futures Reauthorization Act of 2024 extends a national program dedicated to congenital heart disease research, surveillance, and public awareness. It amends the Public Health Service Act to continue funding for these activities from fiscal years 2025 through 2029, replacing the prior authorization that ended in 2024.

The law also introduces new planning and reporting duties for the Secretary of Health and Human Services. Within two years of enactment, the Secretary must provide a report to the House Energy and Commerce Committee and the Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee. That report will describe past and current Department efforts to educate the public about congenital heart disease, including attention to patients’ lifelong needs; assess activities aimed at improving health care provider training on the condition; and detail the existing workforce capacity of providers who care for adults living with congenital heart disease.

Within one year after submitting that report, the Secretary must develop and deliver to Congress a strategy for strengthening public awareness and provider education. The strategy must include findings and recommendations to address gaps in awareness and research tied to lifelong needs—such as long-term health outcomes, quality of life, mental health, and health care utilization—and to tackle workforce shortages among providers treating adult congenital heart disease patients, potentially through enhanced fellowship or continuing education programs. It must also encourage collaboration and information sharing across federal agencies, health care providers, researchers, and patient organizations. In crafting the strategy, the Secretary is required to consult with relevant stakeholders, including patient groups, health professionals, research entities, health insurers, accrediting bodies, and federal agencies like the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, the National Institutes of Health, and the Health Resources and Services Administration.

These additions do not alter the program’s core functions but establish new reporting and strategic planning steps to guide future efforts.
